Unlike SparkBot (CLAUDE.md documents a 7-layer idempotency with in-memory mutex + sparkbot_dedup_locks), the lead path has NO per-(agent,contact) lock around the LLM call. Dominant trigger is per-webhook waitUntil(sleep(debounce+2s)→processMessageQueue()) (route.ts:773-783); cron runs only daily (vercel.json '0 0 * * *'). Race: msg A is claimed and processGroup runs the LLM (~5-15s incl. GHL fetch+retries+carrier RAG+compressHistory). During that window a late inbound B for the SAME contact (fast typer, or GHL+Stevo dual-delivery with DIFFERENT ghl_message_id so the 23505 dedup at route.ts:733 does NOT catch it) inserts as pending and its own waitUntil fires another processMessageQueue that claims B into a fresh independent MessageGroup for the same contact → a SECOND executeActions → two outbound messages to the lead, often contradictory. ghl_message_id UNIQUE (00021) only dedups the identical webhook retry. message_count/last_ai_response_at are written only at the end (action-executor.updateConversationState), so neither overlapping run sees the other.

gaps.area: ai_paused_at read once at top of processGroup, never re-checked before send → bot talks over a human who took over mid-LLM-call (atropela handoff)
gaps.why: convState (with ai_paused_at) is fetched once at queue-processor.ts:303-313 and the pause gate evaluated immediately. The LLM + GHL round-trips then take several seconds. The F52 block explicitly re-reads ai_resumed_at FRESH right before deciding (lines 631-643, 'Race GU-6×F52') precisely because convState is stale — but it does NOT re-read ai_paused_at. So if a human replies during the LLM call (outbound webhook route.ts:476-493 sets ai_paused_at) or the rep clicks pause (contact-pause route), the in-memory convState still shows not-paused and executeActions sends the AI reply ON TOP of the human. The whole purpose of auto_pause_on_human_message is defeated in the exact window it matters. Fix: re-SELECT ai_paused_at (same fresh-read pattern already used for ai_resumed_at) immediately before executeActions and abort if set.

gaps.area: ai_resumed_at permanently disables targeting for a contact (no expiry) → agent answers contacts that no longer match (responder pro contato errado)
gaps.why: queue-processor.ts:380-381 sets manuallyResumed = !!convState.ai_resumed_at and, when true, SKIPS the entire targeting gate (lines 381-410). ai_resumed_at is set by contact-activate route and is ONLY cleared by an explicit manual pause (contact-pause:65, contact-activate:84). No time bound. So once a rep ever clicks 'passa a bola pra IA' on a contact, that contact bypasses targeting_rules forever — even months later when its tag/stage/custom-field no longer qualifies. Combined with follow-ups/reactive triggers re-engaging, the agent can keep messaging a contact targeting was meant to exclude. Likely intentional override, but the unbounded lifetime is a latent wrong-audience vector worth a TTL or re-evaluation.

gaps.area: lead-history recent_messages never sorted — last_human_outbound_at / last_inbound_at trust GHL desc order; wrong order silences the bot (calar a IA)
gaps.why: lead-history.ts:247 builds recent_messages via allMsgs.slice(0, msgsCount) with NO .sort() on dateAdded. last_human_outbound_at (line 297) and last_inbound_at (line 298) then use .find() — correct ONLY if GHL returns strictly newest-first. The file's own comments acknowledge GHL message shape 'varia por canal/versão' (source missing on some channels). If any channel returns ascending/mixed order, .find() returns the OLDEST human outbound. should-respond.ts:60-69 (skip_if_human_replied_within_minutes) keys off last_human_outbound_at: a stale pick reading as recent → spurious 'human_replied_recently' SKIP → bot goes mute on an active lead; or an old human msg read as recent suppresses a needed reply. Contrast queue-processor.ts:561-564 and 595-597 which DO explicitly .sort() the same GHL messages — lead-history should mirror that.

gaps.area: Handoff notification silently degrades to web-only badge when rep has no WhatsApp opt-in → lead asked for a human and nobody is told (handoff dropado)
gaps.why: When should-respond decides SKIP+notify_rep (e.g. lead_requested_human), queue-processor.ts:860-874 calls notifyRepViaSparkbot → deliverProactiveMessage. That function (whatsapp-delivery.ts:114-159) checks hasOptedInViaWhatsApp(rep.id); if the rep only ever used the Web UI, it does NOT send WhatsApp — it persists a 'system' badge and returns {ok:true, via:'system'}. handoff-notify.ts:240 treats ok:true as notified=true, so no error/signal is raised. Net: the lead was NOT answered (bot skipped) AND the rep gets no push — only a web badge they may never open. Worst handoff outcome (lead explicitly requested a human) failing silently. The reportError path in handoff-notify fires only on a thrown delivery error, not on this blocked_no_optin soft-success.

gaps.area: Targeting is fail-OPEN at the processor but fail-CLOSED at the webhook → inconsistent gating; non-matching contact answered when GHL flakes
gaps.why: Two targeting evaluators with OPPOSITE failure modes. webhook checkTargetingRules (route.ts:799-850) is fail-CLOSED ('FAIL CLOSED: returns false on error', line 846), used to ROUTE a brand-new contact to an agent. queue/targeting.ts checkContactMatchesTargeting is fail-OPEN (lines 166-173) and is the runtime gate deciding whether to respond. So for an existing conversation_state contact, if the GHL contact/opps fetch errors at processing time, targeting.ts returns ok:true and the agent answers a contact that may not match. The two also differ in matching (webhook: exact tag includes() and custom_field ===; targeting.ts: case/trim-normalized) — a contact could route under one semantics then be evaluated under another. Low severity (fail-open is documented intent to avoid a mute agent) but the divergence is a correctness/consistency smell worth unifying or commenting.

gaps.area: pg_cron drives the real-time pipeline by POSTing a HARDCODED prod URL + secret stored in a Supabase table (cron_config), not in vercel.json or env
gaps.why: vercel.json only schedules 2 DAILY crons (process-queue @ 0 0, refresh-ghl-token @ 0 6). Everything time-sensitive — sparkbot-proactive (30s), billing-retry (5min), process-message-queue, TTL cleanups — is driven by pg_cron inside Supabase, which net.http_post()s to https://spark-ai-platform.vercel.app with the bearer pulled from the cron_config table (migrations 00070, 00086) or the app.cron_secret GUC (00041). At cutover these are decoupled from the app: if the deployment domain changes, or CRON_SECRET is rotated in Vercel without UPDATE-ing cron_config.proactive_secret / the GUC, pg_cron keeps hitting a stale URL or sends a stale Bearer and every call 401s (isAuthorizedCron rejects), so proactives/billing-retry/queue-draining silently stop. /api/health only watches bulk_runner_health.last_tick, which is downstream of the same cron — if pg_cron dies you get a generic degraded with no pointer to root cause. NOT COVERED: a cutover step to update cron_config.base_url+secret atomically with any domain/secret change, and an independent pg_cron heartbeat alert.

gaps.area: Lead-facing queue dedup reads ONLY body.id; if the new app keys the message id differently, ghl_message_id goes null and DB dedup vanishes
gaps.why: message_queue dedup relies on a PARTIAL unique index WHERE ghl_message_id IS NOT NULL (migration 00021 / SETUP.sql:202). When ghl_message_id is null there is ZERO DB-level idempotency. The inbound route sets it from (body.id as string) || null ONLY (src/app/api/webhooks/inbound-message/route.ts:717) — no fallback to messageId/message_id. The SparkBot handler instead uses (body.messageId || body.message_id || body.id) (webhook-handler.ts:164). So a new app delivering the id under messageId would keep SparkBot deduping but give EVERY lead-facing message a null id. The queue reaper resets status processing->pending after 5min (queue-processor.ts:90-99) with the comment 'Idempotencia via ghl_message_id UNIQUE evita double-send' — with null ids there is no backstop, so a slow/timed-out group is re-claimed and the contact gets a duplicate AI reply. This is the exact 'dedup quando ghl_message_id e null' risk, invisible until a real lead is double-messaged. NOT COVERED: aligning lead-facing id extraction to the same 3-key fallback, plus a captured sample of the NEW app inbound payload to confirm the id key.

gaps.area: Double-app webhook fan-out: dedup between the two GHL apps hinges on matching ghl_message_id, and the cross-app fallbacks differ by pipeline
gaps.why: During the migration window BOTH old and new apps are subscribed to the same locations, so each inbound fires twice. If both apps emit the SAME ghl_message_id, the partial unique index catches it. If they emit DIFFERENT ids (each app generating its own), the lead-facing path has NO content/timing fallback — it enqueues both and replies twice (the 7-layer content/timing dedup in webhook-handler.ts is SparkBot-only; lead-facing has only the unique-index check at route.ts:733). Even for SparkBot, layer-1 in-memory mutex and the hub cache are per-lambda and won't coordinate two apps on two lambdas (only the sparkbot_dedup_locks DB layers survive). There is an isStevoInboundPrimary() guard for the GHL-vs-Stevo double but NO equivalent guard for old-app-vs-new-app. NOT COVERED: a deterministic 'one app is authoritative' switch for the overlap window (e.g. drop inbound whose source/app-id isn't the active app), and confirmation of whether the two apps share message ids.

gaps.area: Token refresh SPOF survives a crash but NOT an app swap: refresh_tokens stored were issued by the OLD client_id; swapping GHL_CLIENT_ID/SECRET invalidates them with no re-consent path wired
gaps.why: refreshAllCompanyTokens (token-refresher.ts) loops every row in the 'Token Refresher' table and POSTs grant_type=refresh_token with the CURRENT process.env.GHL_CLIENT_ID/SECRET. Those refresh_tokens were minted under the OLD app. The moment GHL_CLIENT_ID is swapped to the new app, every refresh returns 4xx (client/token mismatch) and the daily cron flips to total-failure. The total-failure alert exists (refresh-ghl-token/route.ts:54-79) but it FIRES AFTER THE FLIP — and recovery requires exchangeAuthCode for each company, which only runs on a fresh marketplace install/auth-code (no batch re-consent endpoint or script is wired). GHLClient retries 401 once via invalidateTokenCache (client.ts:48-55) but that re-derives a location token from the SAME dead company token, so it cannot self-heal an app swap. Result: ~24h after cutover (token TTL) the entire data plane — SparkBot + all lead agents — starts 401ing on /oauth/locationToken. NOT COVERED: re-OAuthing every company under the new client_id BEFORE swapping env, and a documented batch re-consent path.

gaps.area: Billing meter/app IDs and the wallet charge path are bound to the OLD app's Marketplace meter and must change at cutover
gaps.why: chargeWallet() (charge.ts:148-197) POSTs /marketplace/billing/charges with GHL_MARKETPLACE_APP_ID + GHL_BILLING_METER_ID, both tied to the OLD app's pricing/meter. After cutover the old appId/meterId will 422/403, so charges throw; the throw is swallowed (catch only reportError 'high') and chargeUnbilledRecords retries forever against the wrong meter — silently accruing uncharged usage (the exact failure 00086 was built to fix, reintroduced by the app swap). Separately, the hard cap is enforced per usage_record (isMonthlyCapReached, default $100) but the bot CONTINUES responding when cap_blocked by design — so during a noisy launch (double webhooks -> double LLM spend) a missing/misconfigured cap means runaway cost on Pedro's side with no upstream throttle. NOT COVERED: swapping GHL_MARKETPLACE_APP_ID/METER_ID to the new app as a gated cutover step, and a launch-window spend alarm independent of the per-account cap.

gaps.area: Inbound webhook has no real origin authentication for the new app (GHL Ed25519 not implemented; the HMAC path is effectively off)
gaps.why: The route documents that GHL uses Ed25519 public-key signatures which are NOT implemented; the only real check is an optional HMAC (GHL_WEBHOOK_SECRET) that GHL doesn't send, so in practice the else-branch runs with just IP rate-limit + cost circuit breaker (inbound-message/route.ts:117-172). A new public app URL is internet-reachable and unauthenticated: anyone who learns it can POST crafted inbound to drive (capped but real) LLM spend, or forge STOP/opt-out and outbound-handoff events (route.ts:330-365, 367-535) to silence agents for arbitrary contacts. The Stevo webhook only validates instanceToken IF STEVO_INSTANCE_TOKEN is set and ships with a 'skip validation during setup' branch (stevo/route.ts:68-80) easy to leave on. NOT COVERED: implementing GHL Ed25519 verification (or at minimum requiring HMAC in prod via WEBHOOK_REQUIRE_SIGNATURE), and confirming STEVO_INSTANCE_TOKEN is set before launch.

gaps.area: Queue draining leans on per-request waitUntil + a pg_cron job that 00041 only re-schedules IF EXISTS (process-batch may simply not be scheduled on a fresh/cutover DB)
gaps.why: The lead-facing queue drains two ways: (1) inline via waitUntil(sleep(debounce)+processMessageQueue) on each inbound (route.ts:773-783), and (2) pg_cron 'process-message-queue' -> /api/agents/process-batch — but 00041 only RE-schedules that job IF EXISTS and never creates it, so on a fresh/cutover environment the cron driver may not exist and draining depends entirely on live inbound keeping its own waitUntil alive. If a debounce-window lambda is evicted (cold start, 60s maxDuration, or a burst from double webhooks), the only recovery is the 5-min reaper — up to ~5min latency and (per the null-id gap) possible double-send on recovery. On Vercel's lower tiers the concurrent-execution and wall-clock limits make background waitUntil drain unreliable under load. NOT COVERED: asserting the process-batch pg_cron job actually exists in prod (SELECT from cron.job), and load-testing the debounce/waitUntil path against the plan's concurrency limits.

gaps.area: No time-ordered cutover + rollback runbook tying re-consent, env swap, webhook re-subscription, pg_cron reconfig, and dedup-authority into one validated sequence
gaps.why: The migration touches >=5 independent control points that must change in a specific order: (a) re-OAuth all companies under the new client_id, (b) swap GHL_CLIENT_ID/SECRET + GHL_MARKETPLACE_APP_ID + GHL_BILLING_METER_ID in Vercel, (c) repoint webhooks old->new app, (d) update cron_config.base_url/secret + app.cron_secret GUC if domain/secret change, (e) pick the dedup-authority for the overlap window. Each has its own failure mode above; in the wrong order they cascade (swap client_id before re-consent kills all tokens; repoint webhooks before verifying the new id-key silently breaks dedup). The codebase has rich per-feature flags (SPARKBOT_INBOUND_PRIMARY, STRICT_CLAUDE_ONLY, AGENT_MOTOR_UNIFIED) but no orchestrating checklist sequencing them, and rollback today means hand-reverting redirects/env/cron across Vercel + Supabase. NOT COVERED: a written cutover+rollback runbook (docs/RUNBOOK.md scope) validated on a staging branch with the new app credentials before the production flip.

gaps.area: Multiple pg_cron definitions for the same jobname exist across migrations — a partial/out-of-order replay at cutover can leave the wrong (leaked-secret) version active
gaps.why: 'sparkbot-proactive' is (re)defined in 00032 (hardcoded Bearer spark-cron-secret-2026 + prod URL), 00041 (GUC-based), 00053 (hardcoded rotated secret), and 00070 (cron_config-based). Each unschedules-then-schedules, so the LAST applied wins — but a partial/out-of-order replay, or a fresh env stopping before 00070, can leave the job pointing at prod with a leaked/old secret (the cross-env auto-trigger 00070 was written to fix). The leaked secret spark-cron-secret-2026 is explicitly burned in cron-auth.ts and rejected unless ALLOW_LEAKED_CRON_SECRET=1, so a stale 00032-version job silently 401s and proactives stop. NOT COVERED: a post-migration assertion of the EXACT active command for every cron.job (jobname, url, secret-source) as a cutover gate, and pruning superseded definitions so a replay can't resurrect them.
